title: Excessive Spatter
description: Diagnose and reduce excessive spatter during welding

start.question: Which welding process are you using?

mig_spatter.question: How would you describe the spatter?

options.label: Fine spray of tiny spatter everywhere

mig_voltage_low.title: Voltage Too Low for Wire Speed
mig_voltage_low.fix:
1. Large spatter balls usually mean voltage is too low relative to wire speed.
2. Increase voltage by 1-2 settings and test.
3. Use the selection chart to find the recommended voltage for your wire speed.
4. You should hear a steady "sizzling bacon" sound — not loud pops.
5. Also check that your gas flow is adequate (20-25 CFH).

mig_voltage_high.title: Voltage Too High
mig_voltage_high.fix:
1. Fine spray spatter often means voltage is too high for the wire speed.
2. Decrease voltage by 1-2 settings.
3. Or increase wire feed speed to match the higher voltage.
4. Check stickout — keep it at 3/8" to 1/2".
5. Excessive stickout increases resistance and creates more spatter.

mig_start_stop.question: Check your technique at arc start.

options.label: I start far from the workpiece

fix_start_technique.title: Improve Arc Start Technique
fix_start_technique.fix:
1. Position the nozzle close to the workpiece before pulling the trigger.
2. Start with the wire almost touching the work surface.
3. Use a slight push or drag angle (15-20°) from the start.
4. Don't wave the gun around at arc start — hold steady.
5. Some spatter at start/stop is normal; anti-spatter spray helps with cleanup.

mig_gas_type.title: Check Shielding Gas Mixture
mig_gas_type.fix:
1. 100% CO2 produces significantly more spatter than argon mixes.
2. Switch to 75% Argon / 25% CO2 for much less spatter on mild steel.
3. 90% Argon / 10% CO2 gives even cleaner welds but less penetration.
4. For aluminum, use 100% Argon.
5. Verify gas flow: too little = porosity and spatter; too much = turbulence and spatter.
6. Optimal flow: 20-25 CFH for most MIG work.

fc_spatter.question: What type of flux-cored wire are you using?

fc_self_spatter.title: Self-Shielded Flux-Core Spatter
fc_self_spatter.fix:
1. Some spatter is normal with self-shielded flux-cored wire — it's inherently more spattery.
2. Use DCEN polarity (electrode negative) — wrong polarity drastically increases spatter.
3. Use a drag (pull) technique at 15-45° angle.
4. Don't exceed recommended wire feed speed for the voltage.
5. Keep stickout at 1/2" to 3/4" (longer than MIG).
6. Use anti-spatter spray on the workpiece and nozzle.

fc_gas_spatter.title: Gas-Shielded Flux-Core Spatter
fc_gas_spatter.fix:
1. Verify polarity: gas-shielded flux-core uses DCEP (electrode positive).
2. Check gas flow: 25-30 CFH with 75/25 or 100% CO2.
3. Reduce voltage if seeing excessive spatter — you may be running too hot.
4. Maintain proper stickout of 1/2" to 3/4".
5. Use drag technique at 15-45° angle.
6. Ensure clean base metal — contamination increases spatter.

fc_check_polarity.title: Check Flux-Core Wire Type and Polarity
fc_check_polarity.fix:
1. Read the wire spool label for the AWS classification.
2. E71T-11 (self-shielded) → DCEN polarity, no gas needed.
3. E71T-1 (gas-shielded) → DCEP polarity, 75/25 or CO2 gas required.
4. WRONG POLARITY is the #1 cause of excessive spatter with flux-cored wire.
5. Check your cable connections against the polarity diagram in the manual.

stick_6011.title: 6011/6010 Electrode Spatter
stick_6011.fix:
1. 6011 and 6010 are inherently high-spatter electrodes — this is normal.
2. They're designed for deep penetration and dirty/rusty metal.
3. Reduce arc length (keep it tight) to minimize spatter.
4. Use a slight weave or whip technique.
5. If clean metal is available, consider 7018 for much less spatter.
6. Anti-spatter spray will help with cleanup.

stick_7018.title: 7018 Electrode Spatter
stick_7018.fix:
1. 7018 should produce minimal spatter — if it's bad, the electrode may be damp.
2. Store 7018 rods in a rod oven at 250°F — moisture causes porosity and spatter.
3. Check polarity: 7018 uses DCEP (electrode positive) on the OmniPro.
4. Keep a short arc length — drag the rod lightly.
5. If electrodes have been exposed to moisture, bake at 500°F for 1 hour.

stick_general.title: General Stick Welding Spatter Reduction
stick_general.fix:
1. Match polarity to your electrode type (check the electrode package).
2. Keep a short arc length — the shorter the arc, the less spatter.
3. Use the correct amperage range for your rod diameter.
4. Check the selection chart for recommended settings.
5. Ensure a solid work clamp connection.
6. Consider switching to 7018 for the lowest spatter on clean mild steel.
